literal: sovereign freedom, autonomy; abstract noun from svatantra
invariant_definition: Sovereign freedom — Trika's central technical term for Śiva's absolute self-determination, the power by which Śiva freely self-veils (assumes the appearance of contraction into limited subjects and objects) and freely self-reveals (in *pratyabhijñā*). *Svātantrya* is structurally distinct from both Advaita's *māyā* (which Trika treats as itself a manifestation of *svātantrya*) and from theistic divine-will doctrines — Śiva's freedom is not exercised on a substrate but is constitutive of reality. The user's position-brief (T7) draws on *svātantrya* directly: 'Kashmir Shaivism's *svātantrya* (Śiva's sovereign freedom INCLUDES freedom to self-veil)' is the resource that allows Maya to be itself a manifestation of the divine.

per_school.definition: *Svātantrya* is Śiva's intrinsic and constitutive freedom — the power to do, undo, and reverse, including the power to self-veil. It is identified with Śiva's *vimarśa*-aspect (Śakti) and is the metaphysical engine of cosmic manifestation. Distinguished from any external 'will' (Śiva's freedom is not directed at any other-substrate; there is no other).

The user's thesis takes *svātantrya* as the conceptual resource for resolving the apparent embarrassment of Advaita *māyā*: the divine is free to self-veil, and this freedom is itself part of its perfection. Maya, ignorance, and bias are *intentional expressions of the divine* (T7, T8).

translator_note: 'Sovereign freedom' or 'absolute autonomy' both acceptable. 'Free will' is wrong (imports Christian theology); Śiva's *svātantrya* is not a faculty exercised among options but the constitutive openness of reality itself.
